--

The most exciting JavaScript feature in years — no more Moment.js, no more Date bugs
If you have ever worked with dates in JavaScript, you know the pain. The old Date object is one of the most broken parts of the language. It was designed 10 years ago in 1995 and has caused developer headaches ever since. The Temporal API, arriving in ES2026, finally fixes all of this.
What Was Wrong With the Old Date Object?
The problems with Date are many and well-known in the JavaScript community. First, months are zero-indexed — January is 0, December is 11. This causes so many off-by-one bugs that developers have written articles specifically warning about it. Second, Date objects are mutable — you can accidentally change a date object that was passed to a function, causing bugs that are incredibly hard to track down. Third, timezone handling is a nightmare. If you needed to work with different time zones, you had to reach for an external library like Moment.js or date-fns. Fourth, there is no built-in way to represent just a date without time, or just a time without a date. Everything is mixed.
Moment.js, the most downloaded date library in npm history, was downloaded over 14 million times per week, just because JavaScript’s built-in Date was so bad. Temporal makes all of that unnecessary.
How Temporal Solves Everything
Temporal gives you several specialized objects instead of one broken one. Each object has a clear, specific purpose — which makes your code much easier to read and reason about.
Temporal.PlainDate — just a date, no time, no timezone confusion.
Temporal.PlainTime — just a time, no date attached.
Temporal.PlainDateTime — date and time together, still no timezone.
Temporal.ZonedDateTime — date, time, AND timezone together.
Temporal.Instant — a single precise moment in time, like a timestamp.
Real Example — Creating and Comparing Dates
// Old way — confusing and error-prone
const old = new Date(2026, 5, 15); // Is this May or June? (It's June — month is 0-indexed!)// New Temporal way — clear and readable
const date = Temporal.PlainDate.from({ year: 2026, month: 6, day: 15 });
console.log(date.toString()); // "2026-06-15" — exactly what you expect// Comparing dates — was impossible cleanly before
const today = Temporal.Now.plainDateISO();
const deadline = Temporal.PlainDate.from('2026-12-31');
const daysLeft = today.until(deadline).days;
console.log(`${daysLeft} days until deadline`);Notice how month: 6 Actually means June — not July like in the old Date system. This alone eliminates one of the most common JavaScript bugs.
Timezone Handling — Finally Works Correctly
// Work with timezones without any external library
const meeting = Temporal.ZonedDateTime.from({
timeZone: 'Asia/Kolkata',
year: 2026, month: 6, day: 28, hour: 10
});// Convert to London time — one line!
const londonTime = meeting.withTimeZone('Europe/London');
console.log(londonTime.hour); // automatically calculatedBefore Temporal, converting between time zones required Moment-timezone or the Intl API with complex manual calculations. Now it is one method call.
Immutability — No More Accidental Mutations
Every Temporal object is immutable. When you “change” a date, you actually get a new object back. The original stays untouched. This eliminates an entire class of bugs where passing a date to a function would accidentally modify it.
// add() returns a NEW date — original is unchanged
const today = Temporal.Now.plainDateISO();
const nextWeek = today.add({ days: 7 });
const nextMonth = today.add({ months: 1 });console.log(today.toString()); // still today — untouched!
console.log(nextWeek.toString()); // 7 days from now
console.log(nextMonth.toString()); // 1 month from nowSummary
The Temporal API is the biggest quality-of-life improvement JavaScript has received in years. It gives you immutable date objects, correct month indexing starting at 1, built-in timezone support, and specialized types for different use cases. If you are using Moment.js or date-fns today, Temporal will eventually replace both, with zero dependencies.
Follow for the next article in this series — JavaScript Iterator Helpers, which make working with large data sets faster and more memory-efficient than ever before!
